concept.title: DITA at a Glance
concept.shortdesc: DITA is designed for teams that need:

conbody.p: Darwin Information Typing Architecture (DITA) is an XML-based standard for writing topic-oriented, reusable technical content and publishing it to multiple outputs (HTML, PDF, help systems, portals, etc.). It’s maintained as an open standard by the OASIS DITA Technical Committee.

section.title: Why DITA exists (the problems it solves)
section.p: DITA is designed for teams that need:

ul.li: Reuse at scale (the same warning/procedure used across products and manuals)
Multiple deliverables from one source (HTML + PDF + embedded help)
Product variants (editions, platforms, regions, customer-specific builds)
Large teams needing structure, consistency, and governance

section.p: The core idea: write small, semantically meaningful modules (topics), assemble them with maps, and publish through a repeatable pipeline.

section.title: The DITA mental model

p.b: Topics: small, typed units of information

section.p: DITA encourages authors to write content as topics: self-contained units that answer a user need. The most common base topic types are:
This style is called information typing: using different structures depending on the user intent.
A DITA map is how you organize and publish:
DITA is built to evolve without breaking. You can extend (specialize) elements and constraints to fit your domain while keeping compatibility with tooling.

section.title: XML basics for DITA authors
section.p: DITA files are written in XML. If you have never worked with XML before, here are the five things you need to know before reading any DITA code:

ol.li:
Elements are the building blocks. They have an opening tag and a closing tag:

This is a paragraph.

My Topic Title The text between the tags is the element's content. The tag name (like p or title) tells the processor what kind of content this is.

Attributes add extra information to an element. They go inside the opening tag as name="value" pairs: <concept id="my-topic" xml:lang="en-US"> Here id and xml:lang are attributes of the concept element.

Nesting means putting elements inside other elements. Indentation shows the hierarchy:
  • First item
  • Second item
The li elements are nested inside the ul element. Not every element can go inside every other element — DITA's rules (defined by its DTD or schema) control what nesting is allowed.

Self-closing tags are for elements with no text content. Instead of <tag></tag>, write <tag/>: <data name="difficulty" value="beginner"/> <indexterm>DITA</indexterm> <!-- has content, so NOT self-closing -->

The DOCTYPE declaration at the top of every DITA file tells the processor which topic type this file uses: <!DOCTYPE concept PUBLIC "-//OASIS//DTD DITA Concept//EN" "concept.dtd"> You do not need to memorize this — copy it from a template. The key part is the word after DOCTYPE (concept, task, reference) which must match your root element.

section.p: That is all the XML you need to start reading and writing DITA. Every code example in this guide uses these five patterns.

section.title: Key building blocks in the spec (what you’ll see in real projects)
section.p: Most topics include:
Task topics add specialized structure:
Reference topics commonly use:

ul.li: <refbody> with sections or a reference table-like structure

section.p: The map primarily uses <topicref> to point to topics:
DITA maps can also use:

ul.li: <topichead>: a heading without a linked topic
<topicgroup>: grouping without appearing in TOC (tool-dependent behavior)

section.p: In real implementations you may see:

ul.li: Domains: add sets of elements (e.g., UI domain, highlighting domain)
Constraints: restrict what’s allowed for a certain doc set
Specialization: define new elements as specializations of existing ones

section.p: Rule of thumb: specialize only when you have a strong semantic or validation need and you can support it in publishing + authoring tools.

section.title: The most important DITA features you’ll actually use
section.p: conref pulls content from another location.
Example (reuse a note):

ul.li: Keep conref targets stable and well-named
Treat shared snippets as “mini APIs” (don’t casually restructure them)

section.p: Keys are defined in the map; topics reference them with keyref.
Why keys matter:

ul.li: Change a URL/product name once in the map → updates everywhere
Reuse topics in different publications with different key definitions
Avoid hard-coding product names and links in topics

section.p: Example (in map):
In a topic:
DITA supports filtering content based on attributes. Common attributes include:
Typical use case: one source, multiple editions (e.g., Windows vs Linux).
In topics:
section.codeblock:
Click Start > Settings.

Open the system settings panel.
section.p: Then apply a filter (often using a DITAVAL file in DITA-OT) to include/exclude.
Governance tip: Define a controlled vocabulary early (what values are allowed) and enforce it.

section.title: A tiny working example (topics + map)
section.codeblock:
What is DITA?

DITA is an XML-based standard for writing topic-oriented content that can be reused and published to multiple formats.

Install the product

Make sure you have administrator access.

 Download the installer. Run the installer and follow the prompts. Verify installation by launching the application.

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!DOCTYPE map PUBLIC "-//OASIS//DTD DITA Map//EN" "map.dtd"> <map> <title>Getting Started</title> <topicref href="../concepts/what-is-dita.dita" type="concept"/> <topicref href="../tasks/install.dita" type="task"/> </map>

section.title: Publishing with DITA Open Toolkit (DITA-OT) (common approach)
section.p: DITA-OT is the most widely used open-source engine for publishing DITA.
Generate HTML5:
Generate PDF (toolchain dependent—usually FO-based):
As doc sets grow, use project files to store:
This makes builds consistent across machines/CI.
Common customization layers include:
Practical advice: start with configuration + small overrides, then grow into plugins only when needed.

section.title: Authoring workflow: how teams actually work with DITA
section.p: A common approach:
section.codeblock: dita/ maps/ guides/ getting-started.ditamap topics/ concepts/ what-is-dita.dita tasks/ install.dita reference/ reuse/ common-notes.dita media/ images/ ditaval/ windows.ditaval linux.ditaval out/
section.p: Many teams use a lifecycle like:

ul.li: File-based: Git + DITA files, fast for engineers/DevOps-heavy teams
CCMS: workflow, reuse management, metadata, translations, governance at enterprise scale

section.p: DITA works in both models; the difference is management tooling.

section.title: Best practices (what prevents pain later)

p.b: Write small topics with a single purpose

ul.li: Don’t write “chapters” as a single topic

p.b: Use keys for anything that changes across deliverables

ul.li: Product names, external URLs, reused references
This keeps topics clean and portable
Overuse can make content hard to read and maintain
Consider “reuse boundaries”: warnings, UI strings, legal text, standard steps
Topic IDs and file names should be stable
Use predictable IDs for conref targets

section.p: - well-formed XML - schema/DTD validation (as applicable) - broken links/conrefs/keys

section.title: Common pitfalls (and how to avoid them)

ul.li: Giant topics → split into focused topics; assemble via maps
Conref spaghetti → limit conref usage; centralize shared content; document ownership
Uncontrolled filtering values → standardize and govern values
Specialization too early → start with base DITA; specialize only with clear requirements

ol.li: Define a starter map set (Getting Started, User Tasks, Reference)
Introduce keys for product name + URLs
Add one reuse file (reuse/common-notes.dita) and use a couple of conrefs
Set up one filtering example (platform=windows/linux) + build it both ways
Automate publishing via a script or CI

section.title: Next steps (if you want to go deeper)
section.p: If you’re aiming for “professional-level DITA” adoption, the next major skills are:

ul.li: Keys + key scopes for multi-publication reuse
Translation workflows (conref/keyref stability matters a lot)

section.title: Analytics and Instrumentation
section.p: Track which introduction topics are most viewed and where users drop off. Use analytics to improve the onboarding experience.

section.title: Personalization and Conditional Processing
section.p: Use conditional content to tailor the introduction for different backgrounds (writer, developer, manager).

section.title: Integrating with Conversational AI and NLP
section.p: Enable users to ask a chatbot “What is DITA?” and receive context-aware answers from this topic.

section.title: Continuous Improvement and Feedback Loops
section.p: Add a prompt for users to rate the introduction and suggest improvements. Use analytics and feedback to refine onboarding content.
